title: Self-Assembled Ultrathin Films: From Layered Nanoarchitectures to Functional Assemblies
abstract:
Research News
Self-Assembled Ultrathin Films:
From Layered Nanoarchitectures
to Functional Assemblies**
By Xi Zhang and Jiacong Shen*
1. Introduction
Recently, self-assembled ultrathin films have received
considerable interest because they allow fabrication of su-
pramolecular assemblies with tailored architecture and
properties.[1] For example, they provide a simple model sys-
tem to investigate the structure±function relationship of
cell membranes;[2] they can also be used to fabricate func-
tional devices based on ultrathin films.[3] All of these appli-
cations rely on the availability of stable interfaces, with a
tight control over parameters such as the thickness at the
monolayer level, the incorporation of chromophores and
their alignments, the surface properties, and the interfacial
diffusion. In this research news, we describe some recent
progress in the study of self-assembled ultrathin films in
our laboratory that covers both layered nanoarchitecture
and functional assemblies. The ultimate goal of this re-
search is to fabricate functional devices through self-assem-
bly and to integrate the material and devices into one unity.
2. Layered Nanoarchitectures
Self-assembled ultrathin films are mainly formed at inter-
faces, e.g., gas/liquid or liquid/solid interfaces. We have
found that the amphiphilic polymer consisting of hydro-
phobic styrene-divinylbenzene microgel and hydrophilic
polyacrylic acid grafting chains was able to self-rearrange
at the air±water interface to form the so-called ªduckweedº
monolayer. By controlling the grafting reaction, some resi-
due double bonds could be left unpolymerized in the mi-
crogel. Although this kind of ªduckweedº is not highly or-
dered, it can be further polymerized to enhance its thermal
stability after it has been transferred onto a solid substrate
using the Langmuir±Blodgett (LB) film technique.[4] We
also found that the amphiphilic polymer composed of hy-
drophilic ethylenediamine-epichlorohydrin slightly cross-
linking network and hydrophobic stearoyl chains could
self-organize at the air±water interface to form the so-
called ªreversed duckweedº monolayer. For an amphiphilic
polymer with Mn » 7840, alkyl chains content » 75.8 %, and
crosslinking degree » 25 %, it can be readily transferred
onto a solid substrate as the LB multilayer with a transfer
ratio of close to unity (Fig. 1A). More than three orders of
sharp diffraction peaks (with odd±even alternative peak in-
tensities) were observed for a polymeric LB film containing
48 layers. Combining with FTIR measurements, we could
conclude that the hydrophilic network was flexible enough
and could reform its configuration to result in a thin, flat
shape at the air±water interface on which hydrophobic al-
kyl chains were closely packed with a highly ordered orien-
tation.[5]
The self-organization of the amphiphilic polymer-bearing
hydrophobic alkyl side chains can be accelerated either by
heating or by slow evaporation of organic solvents. The
structures of the thermotropic mesophases were character-
ized as a function of the grafting content of long alkyl
chains by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), temper-
ature dependent X-ray diffraction.[6] It is inferred that
some hydrophobic semi-rigid rods formed due to the strong
interaction between the long alkyl chains, and thus thermo-
tropic mesophases formed from of these amphiphilic poly-
mers although they contain no mesogenic groups. Casting
film from aqueous bilayer membranes is another alterna-
tive route to multilayer films as proposed by Kunitake.[7]
We found that an ordered self-organizing film of the am-
phiphilic polymer could be formed on solid substrates like
Si single crystal or mica by slow evaporation of organic sol-
vents (Fig. 2).[8] We believe that supramolecular organiza-
tion in the macromolecular system leads to the ordered
film with a partially interdigitated layer structure different
from the corresponding bulk materials (Fig. 1B).
Alternating physisorption of opposite charged polyions
at liquid/solid interface is a layer-by-layer assembly tech-
nique that was first introduced by Decher in 1991.[9] Due to
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 13
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/1309-1139 $ 17.50+.50/0
1139
±
[*] Prof. J. Shen, Prof. X. Zhang
Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Spectroscopy
Department of Chemistry, Jilin University
Changchun, Jilin 130023 (P. R. China)
[**] This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of
China.
 1140
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/1309-1140 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 13
many advantages of this method, such as easiness in fabrica-
tion, independence of substrate size and topology, good me-
chanical and chemical stability of the resulting film, it has
been recognized that the layer-by-layer assembling tech-
nique is a powerful and versatile means for assembling mul-
ticomposite supramolecular structures with a good control
over the layer composition and thickness. We have used
poly(maleic acid monoester) and cationic bis(pyridinium)
salt bearing biphenyl mesogenic group to produce as-
sembled multilayer films.[10] The driving force in this case is
electrostatic attraction between polyions and bication, com-
bined with Van der Waals interactions among mesogenic
groups of bications. As for polycation/polyanion systems,
their interfaces are usually interpenetrated, e.g. with inter-
digitation of the order of 1.2 nm for poly(ethylene-imine)/
poly(styrene-4-sulfonate) (PEI/PSS).[11] In the case of bicat-
ion/polyanion multilayer systems, we found there existed
Bragg diffractions, an indication of improvement of the in-
terfacial interpenetrating to some extent. Because there are
strong acid sulfonate groups in side chains and weak acid
carboxyl groups in main chains, adjustment of pH value of
cationic solution has a remarkable effect on the supra-
molecular structure of the multilayer assemblies. Two types
of ultrathin films were therefore fabricated with either qua-
si-symmetric or quasi-asymmetric structures (Fig. 1C).
Fabrication of planar molecules such as porphyrin or
phthalocyanine into supramolecular arrays remains to be a
great challenge, though study on their LB films has made
tremendous progress. Multilayer buildup of these planar
functional molecules was obtained through alternate de-
position of meso-tetra(4-sulfonyl) porphyrin(TPPS4) or
copper phthalocyaninetetrasulfonic acid, tetrasodium salt
(CuTsPc) with cationic bis(pyridinium) salt based on elec-
trostatic attraction.[12] One step further, multilayer assem-
blies of porphyrin and phthalocyanine can also be prepared
by alternating deposition of oppositely charged rigid planar
molecules of sodium (phthalocyanine tetra-sulfonate)co-
balt Na4[(CoTsPc)] and tetrakis(N,N,N-trimethyl-4-anilin-
ium)porphyrin cobalt [(CoTAP)]Br4 (Fig. 1D).[13]
The driving force for the alternate assembly need not to
be electrostatic. In fact, the alternate assembly has been re-
alized by taking advantage of hydrogen bonding between
Research News
Fig. 1. Illustration of layered nanoarchitectures: A) Reversed ªDuckweedº LB film. B) Self-organizing film with interdigitated structure. C) Electrostatic multi-
layer assemblies of polyelectrolyte and bication. D) Electrostatic multilayered assemblies of porphyrin and phthalocyanine. E) Multilayer assemblies of PAA
and PVP based on hydrogen bonding. F) Multilayer assemblies of PSS(Cu2+)1/2 and PVP based on coordination.

Self-Assembled Ultrathin Films:
From Layered Nanoarchitectures

considerable interest because they allow fabrication of su-

ized as a function of the grafting content of long alkyl

tive route to multilayer films as proposed by Kunitake.[7]

tion, independence of substrate size and topology, good me-
chanical and chemical stability of the resulting film, it has
been recognized that the layer-by-layer assembling tech-
nique is a powerful and versatile means for assembling mul-
ticomposite supramolecular structures with a good control
over the layer composition and thickness. We have used
poly(maleic acid monoester) and cationic bis(pyridinium)
salt bearing biphenyl mesogenic group to produce as-
sembled multilayer films.[10] The driving force in this case is
electrostatic attraction between polyions and bication, com-
bined with Van der Waals interactions among mesogenic
groups of bications. As for polycation/polyanion systems,
their interfaces are usually interpenetrated, e.g. with inter-
digitation of the order of 1.2 nm for poly(ethylene-imine)/

molecular structure of the multilayer assemblies. Two types
of ultrathin films were therefore fabricated with either qua-
si-symmetric or quasi-asymmetric structures (Fig. 1C).

phthalocyanine into supramolecular arrays remains to be a
great challenge, though study on their LB films has made
tremendous progress. Multilayer buildup of these planar
functional molecules was obtained through alternate de-
position of meso-tetra(4-sulfonyl) porphyrin(TPPS4) or
copper phthalocyaninetetrasulfonic acid, tetrasodium salt
(CuTsPc) with cationic bis(pyridinium) salt based on elec-
trostatic attraction.[12] One step further, multilayer assem-
blies of porphyrin and phthalocyanine can also be prepared
by alternating deposition of oppositely charged rigid planar
molecules of sodium (phthalocyanine tetra-sulfonate)co-
balt Na4[(CoTsPc)] and tetrakis(N,N,N-trimethyl-4-anilin-
ium)porphyrin cobalt [(CoTAP)]Br4 (Fig. 1D).[13]
The driving force for the alternate assembly need not to
be electrostatic. In fact, the alternate assembly has been re-
alized by taking advantage of hydrogen bonding between
Fig. 1. Illustration of layered nanoarchitectures: A) Reversed ªDuckweedº LB film. B) Self-organizing film with interdigitated structure. C) Electrostatic multi-
layer assemblies of polyelectrolyte and bication. D) Electrostatic multilayered assemblies of porphyrin and phthalocyanine. E) Multilayer assemblies of PAA
and PVP based on hydrogen bonding. F) Multilayer assemblies of PSS(Cu2+)1/2 and PVP based on coordination.
poly(4-vinylpyridine) and its complementary partner of
polyacrylic acid (Fig. 1E), or between polyaniline and poly-
ethyleneglycol oxide.[14] The Kiessig fringes of X-ray dif-
fraction were well resolved and they suggested a good and
smooth film with a constant thickness and a low surface
roughness, without regular electron density modulation
across the film. Multilayer film can also be fabricated based
on coordinating bonds, e.g. a self-organizing film via con-
secutive adsorption of poly(copper styrene-4-sulfonate)
and poly(4-vinylpyridine).[15] The specific coordination
between the pyridine and the Cu2+ is responsible for build-
ing up the multilayer (Fig. 1F). Earlier work on the self-
assembled multilayers was reported by Mallouk by the use
of coordination of phosphate to Zr4+ ion.[16]
Water-soluble proteins or enzymes can be induced to be
charged species similar to polycations or polyanions for
preparation of ordered macromolecular assemblies. The in-
corporation of proteins by alternative electrostatic adsorp-
tion was realized for a cationic bispyridinium salt and glu-
cose isomerase.[17] The glucose isomerase intercalated
between bispyridinium salt still kept its activity in the mul-
tilayer. Fabrication of ultrathin films containing bienzyme
of glucose oxidase and glucoamylase based on electrostatic
attraction was also achieved on the surface of a gold elec-
trode and its application as a bienzyme maltose sensor was
demonstrated (Fig. 3A).[18] In order to facilitate the elec-
tron transfer among the layers, electroactive polyeletro-
lytes, poly(vinylpyridine)salt bearing Os complex was in-
oxidase.[19] Furthermore, no inherent restriction concerning
non-planar supports and large scale application of the as-
sembling technique make it possible to be used for enzyme
immobilization in porous polymer carrier. We have suc-
cessfully immobilized two layers of glucose isomerase in
the beads of porous trimethylamine polystyrene by using
2338 I.U. per gram of dry gel; this value is almost twice that
of the activity of immobilized monolayer glucose isomer-
ase, indicating that the new immobilization method can in-
crease the enzyme loading in the carriers and then enhance
Due to the strong adhesion of adjacent layers and the in-
dependence of the procedure on the substrate topology,
chemically modified electrodes prepared in this way may
open new horizon for sensors, in particular, in combination
with flow-injection analysis. For example, CuTsPc-modified
gold electrode showed selective potentiometric response to
Cu2+ ion in the range from 1 ´ 10±5 to 0.1 M.[21] CoTsPc-
modified gold electrode exhibited an electro-catalytic re-
sponse for oxidation of carbohydrate and hydrazine, and
therefore it can be used as amperometric sensors for these
150 pmol injected for glucose or fructose to 600 pmol in-
jected for maltose or sucrose.[22] The linear range extends
from 1 ´ 10±3 to 1 ´ 10±6 M for hydrazine (Fig. 3B).[23] The
chemically modified electrode with alternating deposition
of 1:12 phosphomolybdic anions and water-soluble polypyr-
role was shown to exhibit an electrocatalytic response for
the reduction of hydrogen peroxide, and thus hydrogen
peroxide was detected by flow-injection analysis with high
sensitivity with a linear range from 1 ´ 10±2 to 1 ´
Some dyes or pigments can not be easily assembled di-
rectly using the alternating deposition technique.[25] Taking
the 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) as an ex-
ample, it is a very interesting material with good lumines-
cence properties. When we use UV-vis spectra to monitor
the alternating deposition process of PTCA with PEI, a zig-
zag curve was found instead of a linear increase of the ab-
sorption band versus number of layers deposited.[26] This
result suggests that a large amount of PTCA was assembled
during the immersion in PTCA solution, but in the next
dipping in PEI solution, only a part of the already as-
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/1309-1141 $ 17.50+.50/0
Fig. 2. X-ray diffraction patterns for a) the self-organizing polymer film by
slow evaporation of solvent, and b) the polymer bulk powder. (*) The theo-
retically calculated intensities of the diffraction peaks.
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/1309-1142 $ 17.50+.50/0
sembled PTCA was retained on the substrate. To resolve
this problem, we mixed the solution of PTCA dye with PSS
first, and the mixed solution was used in alternating assem-
bly with PEI (Fig. 4). In doing so, stable multilayer assem-
blies were fabricated (Fig. 3C). The electroluminescent de-
vice of 20 layers of PEI/(PSS+PTCA) assembled film as
the emitting layer was fabricated on ITO electrode (as the
anode), and then covered with aluminum cathode. A green
light was observed with a turn-on voltage about 4.7 V. In-
terestingly, it is found that the incorporation of dyes into
PSS matrix as well as PEI inert layer can partially eliminate
the luminescence quenching of dye in the assembling film.
Organizing inorganic nanoparticles into polymer matrix
is a way to realize advanced materials of nanocomposite.[27]
Two approaches have been developed in our lab: 1) Prep-
aration of colloidal particles, and then alternating deposi-
tion of colloidal particles and bipolar compounds or poly-
electrolytes; 2) Preparation of self-organizing polymer film
via coordination and then in-situ formation of heterostruc-
ture film by chemical reaction in restricted area. PbI2 hy-
drosol,[28] CdS particle,[29] Ag-doped ZnS,[30] and TiO2/PbS
coupled particles (Fig. 3D)[31] have been successfully as-
sembled into multilayer assemblies via the layer-by-layer
technique. Based on results from UV-vis spectra, TEM,
and AFM measurements, it is evident that nanoparticle ag-
gregation is effectively prohibited, thus unique properties
of nanoscale particles are retained after assembling into
the multilayers. As mentioned previously, PSS(Cu)1/2/PVP
self-organizing film can be fabricated on the basis of coor-
dination bonding. Exposure of the PSS(Cu)1/2/PVP multi-
layer film to H2S gas is to prepare in situ heterostructured
around 370 nm in the multilayer film after reaction indicat-
Fig. 3. Illustration of functional assembling systems: A) Enzyme sensor. B) Chemically modified electrode. C) Light emitting diode. D) Photocurrent generation
Fig. 4. UV-vis absorption spectra of multilayered PEI/(PSS+PTCA) films
with different numbers of layers. From the lower to upper curves, the num-
ber of layers is 4, 8, 12, and 16, respectively. The inset shows the absorbance
at 222.5 nm and 475 nm versus the number of bilayers.
ing the formation of Q-Cu2S in the multilayered film.[15]
Recently, we also successfully fabricated organized hetero-
structured thin film containing CdS and ZnS nanoparticles
using a similar procedure.[32]
In summary, we have briefly discussed interesting phe-
nomena and potential applications self-assembled ultrathin
films, mainly based on our work. In some cases, we have
prepared sharp and stable interface with ordered struc-
tures. There is still a long way to bridge the gap between
nanoarchitecture and function. The integration of nano-
architecture and function in one self-assembling system is
probably the way that will eventually solve the problems
encountered in device fabrication. We believe that macro-
molecules based thin films enrich the diversity and provide
stability of the resulting supramolecular assemblies; re-
search in this regard has not only fundamental interest but
